::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)

eras_data <- read_xlsx("../../data/08-TS_data.xlsx")
```
:::


<!--
Note: edit path above to read straight from GitHub?
-->

Let's explore factor variables in R! Work through these questions, running or editing the code provided to help you answer them.


1. What are the two general purposes for factor variables (in R)?



### `fct()`

2. The `fct()` function in **forcats** serves the same purpose as what function in base R?

3. How are the levels automatically ordered with the `fct()` function? How is this different from base R?



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
eras_data |> 
  mutate(Album = fct(Album)) |> 
  pull(Album)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 [1] Lover      Lover      Lover      Lover      Lover      Lover     
 [7] Fearless   Fearless   Fearless   Evermore   Evermore   Evermore  
[13] Evermore   Evermore   Reputation Reputation Reputation Reputation
[19] Speak Now  Speak Now  Red        Red        Red        Red       
[25] Folklore   Folklore   Folklore   Folklore   Folklore   Folklore  
[31] Folklore   1989       1989       1989       1989       1989      
[37] Red        Speak Now  Midnights  Midnights  Midnights  Midnights 
[43] Midnights  Midnights  Midnights 
9 Levels: Lover Fearless Evermore Reputation Speak Now Red Folklore ... Midnights
```


:::
:::


4. Edit the code above to just output the **levels** of the `Album` variable.

5. What does including the `levels = ` argument do in `fct()`?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
eras_data |> 
  mutate(Album = fct(Album,
                     levels = c("Fearless","Speak Now","Red",
                                "1989", "Reputation","Lover",
                                "Folklore", "Evermore","Midnights"))) |> 
  pull(Album)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 [1] Lover      Lover      Lover      Lover      Lover      Lover     
 [7] Fearless   Fearless   Fearless   Evermore   Evermore   Evermore  
[13] Evermore   Evermore   Reputation Reputation Reputation Reputation
[19] Speak Now  Speak Now  Red        Red        Red        Red       
[25] Folklore   Folklore   Folklore   Folklore   Folklore   Folklore  
[31] Folklore   1989       1989       1989       1989       1989      
[37] Red        Speak Now  Midnights  Midnights  Midnights  Midnights 
[43] Midnights  Midnights  Midnights 
9 Levels: Fearless Speak Now Red 1989 Reputation Lover Folklore ... Midnights
```


:::
:::


5. What happens if you forget to provide an album to `levels = ` that exists in the data in the code above?



### `fct_recode()`

6. What does this function do? When would you want to use it?


7. What happens to non-specified levels in `fct_recode()`?


8. Change the level "Red" to "Red!" and "Fearless" to "Fearless - Taylors Version" in the `Album` variable:


::: {.cell}

:::



### `fct_collapse()`

9. What does this function do?  When would you want to use it?




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
eras_data |> 
  mutate(Genre = fct_collapse(.f= Album,
                       "country pop" = c("Fearless"),
                       "pop rock" = c("Speak Now","Red"),
                       "electropop" = c("1989","Reputation","Lover"),
                       "folk pop" = c("Folklore","Evermore"),
                       "alt-pop" = "Midnights"))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 45 × 5
   Song                                   Album    Length Single Genre      
   <chr>                                  <chr>     <dbl> <chr>  <fct>      
 1 Miss Americana & the Heartbreak Prince Lover      3.9  N      electropop 
 2 Cruel Summer                           Lover      2.97 Y      electropop 
 3 The Man                                Lover      3.17 Y      electropop 
 4 You Need to Calm Down                  Lover      2.85 Y      electropop 
 5 Lover                                  Lover      3.68 Y      electropop 
 6 The Archer                             Lover      3.52 N      electropop 
 7 Fearless                               Fearless   4.03 Y      country pop
 8 You Belong With Me                     Fearless   3.85 Y      country pop
 9 Love Story                             Fearless   3.92 Y      country pop
10 'tis the damn season                   Evermore   3.82 N      folk pop   
# ℹ 35 more rows
```


:::
:::


10. Why would you want to use `fct_collapse()` rather than `case_when()`? 


11. What happens to levels of the variable `.f = ` that you do not include in `fct_collapse()`?
 

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
eras_data |> 
  mutate(genre = fct_collapse(.f= Album,
                       "country pop" = c( "Fearless"),
                       "pop rock" = c("Speak Now","Red"),
                       "electropop" = c("1989","Reputation","Lover"),
                       "folk pop" = c("Folklore","Evermore")
                       )) |> 
  pull(genre) |> 
  levels()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "electropop"  "folk pop"    "country pop" "Midnights"   "pop rock"   
```


:::
:::

 
12. Edit the code below to fix this, so that the Midnights album is categorized as "other" in the `genre` variable.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
eras_data |> 
  mutate(genre = fct_collapse(.f= Album,
                       "country pop" = c( "Fearless"),
                       "pop rock" = c("Speak Now","Red"),
                       "electropop" = c("1989","Reputation","Lover"),
                       "folk pop" = c("Folklore","Evermore")
                       )) |> 
  pull(genre) |> 
  levels()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "electropop"  "folk pop"    "country pop" "Midnights"   "pop rock"   
```


:::
:::

 
 
### `fct_relevel()` 

13. What does this function do?  When would you want to use it?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
eras_data |> 
  mutate(Album = fct_relevel(.f = Album, 
                             c("Fearless","1989","Taylor Swift",
                               "Speak Now","Red","Midnights","Reputation"))) |>
  pull(Album) |>
  levels()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Fearless"   "1989"       "Speak Now"  "Red"        "Midnights" 
[6] "Reputation" "Evermore"   "Folklore"   "Lover"     
```


:::
:::


14. What happens with levels that are not specified?



### `fct_infreq()`

15. What does `fct_infreq()` do?  When would you want to use it?



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
eras_data |> 
  ggplot() +
  geom_bar(aes(y = fct_infreq(Album)), 
           fill = "#A5C9A5") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Number of Songs",
       y = "",
       subtitle = "Album",
       title = "Songs Played on the Eras Tour")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](08-code-along_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


### `fct_reorder()`

16. What does `fct_reorder()` do?  In what setting would you use it?



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# you may need to install this package!
library(ggridges)

eras_data |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = Length, 
             y = fct_reorder(.f = Album,
                             .x = Length,
                             .fun = mean), 
             fill = Album)) +
  geom_density_ridges() +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")+
  labs(x = "Song Length (mins)",
       y = "",
       subtitle = "Album",
       title = "Songs Played on the Eras Tour")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](08-code-along_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::



### `fct_reorder2()`

17. What does `fct_reorder2()` do?  In what setting would you use it?


18. What are the arguments to `fct_reorder2()`?


19. Let's improve a plot of the number of listings over time for five cities in Texas. Use `fct_reorder2()` to reorder the legend below so that the legend aligns with the order of the lines in the plot.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
data(txhousing)

txhousing |>
  filter(city %in% c("Dallas","Fort Worth", "Austin",
                     "Houston", "El Paso"),
         !is.na(listings)) |> 
  ggplot() +
  geom_line(mapping = aes(x = date, 
                          y = listings,
                          color = city))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](08-code-along_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::
